title: Lesson 345
transcript: OK, we're in domain number four, which, as you know, is focused on network security. How do we protect the network and its services? And in this section, we've been talking about really how there are a whole bunch of tools that are available to, for example, attackers. and those that like to exploit things and so on. One of the focuses of network security needs to be that we can use the same tools to really understand the exploits and vulnerabilities that we may be prone to so that again we can implement security controls to gap those vulnerabilities. So the same tools that are available to attackers are the tools that our security professionals should be using in order to be able to protect the network and its services and segments and assets and so on. So what are some of these tools? Well, the exam doesn't require you to understand each of these to a deep level. The whole focus of this entire video and section is that we should be using these within our organization, within our network, so that we can understand potentially where certain exploits and vulnerabilities may exist so things like ipconfig and using ping for example to see what a host returns that again may be valuable to attackers and so on doing a trace route command which tries to map for example what network elements look like between two points and so on which is again very valuable to attackers and some of the other ones that you see here again not important to understand specifically each of these from an exam perspective But overall, these are tools that can be useful to us as well. And some of these are obviously very important to be able to use. And what we've got over the next series of slides here are just examples, again, just to give you an idea of the type of information that these may return. So here's IP config for you. And then we've got ping, which tries to see if a host is alive And operating, for example, which again might provide some very valuable information to potential attackers. One of the things that we talked about is that You know, there are phases that an attack has to go through successfully to ultimately be a successful attack. And the first phase of this, as you know, is what's called reconnaissance. And then the next phase is what's called enumeration. Well, some of these tools can be useful to provide valuable information to complete those phases. And so that attackers may be able to determine, for example, that you are a attractive target because they find information here related to, for example, you know doing reconnaissance and enumeration to understand some of the exploits that may be possible within your organization and so on and that includes doing commands like who is and even dig and so on right But the one thing that we also have to understand is that there are also automated tools that attackers can use that try and automate this entire process of not only looking for services and open ports, for example, but also looking deeper than that for specific exploits and vulnerabilities that may be possible against some of your infrastructures and architectures. And so again, these are tools like these that you see on the slide, like Nmap and Nessus and so on. These are automated ways to provide, for example, really valuable information about exploits and vulnerabilities that may exist. The whole point of this is that we, as part of network security, should be using the same tools on a regular basis to understand what vulnerabilities and exploits we need to be able to gap and fix so that real attackers cannot use them and exploit them.
